planting.note: Plant nursery-grown divisions in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in early fall or late winter; lenten rose establishes most reliably from container-grown plants rather than bare-root divisions and resents frequent transplanting once established — site carefully.

directions: Plant container-grown divisions in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; lenten rose (Helleborus × hybridus) is one of the most valuable and irreplaceable shade garden perennials in temperate gardens — an evergreen clump of bold, leathery, dark-green, deeply palmate leaves that produces nodding, cup-shaped flowers in an extraordinary color range (pure white, cream, pale yellow, soft pink, rose, deep magenta, plum, near-black, and every bicolor and picotee combination) from late winter through early spring, often blooming through snow. Unlike most spring flowers it opens during the coldest months of late winter, providing garden color at the bleakest time of year. The flowers face downward, so tilt them gently upward to appreciate the intricate inner markings — spotted, veined, and blotched patterns of stunning complexity are characteristic of the named hybrid selections. Plants are long-lived (decades), deer-resistant, slug-resistant, and self-seed freely to form expanding colonies. 'Wedding Party Series', 'Winter Jewels Series', and 'Frostkiss Series' offer named selections in predictable colors. Do not cut old foliage until new growth emerges in late winter. An absolute cornerstone plant for the shade garden.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 12–16 weeks before last frost (seeds are nearly microscopic — surface-sow on moistened germinating mix and press lightly, do not cover) or plant container-grown transplants after all frost danger has passed in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in partial shade.

directions: Start seeds indoors 12–16 weeks before last frost (surface-sow only — seeds require light for germination) or plant transplants after frost in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in partial shade; wax begonia is the most widely grown shade-tolerant annual, producing an uninterrupted cascade of small, neat, single or double flowers in red, pink, salmon, coral, orange, or white on compact, self-cleaning mounds of glossy, waxy, green or bronze-tinted foliage from transplant to killing frost. Unlike tuberous begonias, wax begonias tolerate significant summer heat and humidity and are far easier to grow — they require almost no attention beyond deadhead-free, continuous bloom. Bronze-leaved selections ('Cocktail Series') handle full sun better than green-leaved forms. Excellent for massed bedding, container work, window boxes, and edging shaded walks and borders. In zones 10–12 they persist as tender perennials; in cooler climates they are lifted and overwintered indoors or discarded after frost. One of the most reliable and widely available bedding plants available.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Divide offsets or plant container-grown specimens in consistently moist, rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade after frost danger has passed; giant taro requires abundant moisture and fertilizer during the growing season and appreciates filtered light — protect from harsh afternoon sun which scorches the enormous leaves.

directions: Divide offsets or plant container specimens in consistently moist, rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; giant taro is the tropical foliage plant of maximum dramatic impact — its enormous, upward-pointing, arrow-shaped leaves can reach 3–4 feet in length on petioles rising to 6–15 feet, creating an unmistakable, prehistoric, jungle-scale effect that no other plant in zone 8–12 gardening can match. The architectural, waxy, deep-green leaves with prominent white midribs create instant tropical drama in pools, patio gardens, and mixed tropical borders. It differs from Colocasia (taro) in that the leaves point upward and outward rather than drooping, and the petioles attach at the base of the leaf rather than near the center. In zone 8, top growth is killed by frost but the root mass often survives if mulched heavily; in zones 9–12 it is evergreen. Spreads by offshoots that can be divided. Corms are edible only after prolonged cooking (toxic raw). 'Variegata' (green and white-striped) and 'Borneo Giant' are selected forms.

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds after last frost or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in partial shade in spring; pigeonberry self-seeds prolifically in suitable shaded areas of Gulf Coast gardens and functions essentially as a self-maintaining native groundcover once established.

directions: Direct-sow or plant container specimens in well-drained to moist, average soil in partial shade after frost; pigeonberry (rouge plant, baby peppers, coralito) is a native perennial sub-shrub of the Gulf South, Caribbean, and Central America that is one of the most wildlife-friendly and adaptable shade-tolerant native plants for zone 7–11 — simultaneously producing clusters of tiny, star-shaped, white to pale-pink flowers and brilliant translucent red-orange berries on the same plant throughout most of the growing season, providing months of ornamental interest and a constant food source for mockingbirds, Carolina wrens, white-eyed vireos, and numerous other fruit-eating birds. The plant is low-growing, semi-woody at the base, and self-seeds freely in shaded garden beds, under trees, and at woodland edges to create ever-expanding naturalized colonies. An excellent native alternative to non-native shade groundcovers, it fills the ecological role of producing berries in shaded conditions where most fruiting plants will not bloom. Berries are considered mildly toxic to humans but are consumed in quantity by birds.

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds in spring or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in partial shade after frost; aristolochia fimbriata is low-growing and spreading — excellent as a small-scale groundcover or container spiller — and is a key host plant for Pipevine Swallowtail butterfly larvae.

directions: Direct-sow or plant container specimens in moist, well-drained, average soil in partial shade; fringed dutchman's pipe is a compact, spreading, South American perennial grown primarily as a wildlife plant and groundcover for shaded areas — the handsome, heart-shaped leaves are distinctly patterned with silver-white veining against deep green, giving the plant attractive foliage interest independent of its flowers. Small, curved, pipe-shaped, lemon-scented flowers with finely fringed edges appear throughout the warm season, but the plant's greatest value is as a confirmed host plant for the Pipevine Swallowtail butterfly (Battus philenor), a stunning iridescent-blue butterfly whose larvae feed exclusively on Aristolochia species. Unlike the larger, climbing Aristolochia macrophylla and A. tomentosa, A. fimbriata stays low and spreading, making it practical for smaller gardens, containers, and shaded garden edges. It is important to note that certain other non-native Aristolochia species (A. elegans, A. littoralis) are actually toxic to Pipevine Swallowtail larvae; A. fimbriata supports them safely. Semi-evergreen in mild winters.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
